name: Nanjing
intro: Nanjing suits travelers who want a historically weighty east-China city with easier pacing than Beijing and a strong mix of museums, walls, republican-era landmarks, and old-city evenings.
seoTitle: Nanjing Travel Guide for First-Time Visitors
seoDescription: Plan a first trip to Nanjing with practical advice on where to stay, how many days to give the city, and which historical sites and evening districts deserve priority.
tripPace: Best when the route wants a thoughtful historical city stop with real evening character and manageable movement.
highlights: One of the easiest east-China cities to add when the trip wants more historical depth without immediately jumping into Beijing's scale.
Works well for travelers who like history expressed through city walls, museums, republican-era sites, and old-city evening districts rather than only through imperial monuments.
Usually feels strongest as an overnight or two-day stop instead of a rushed check-the-box rail excursion.

planningPriorities.title: Decide whether Nanjing is a real stop or a rushed side trip
planningPriorities.description: That one choice changes whether the city feels layered and rewarding or like a compressed historical sampler.

planningPriorities.title: Build the stay around old city or central convenience
planningPriorities.description: Hotel area matters because Nanjing evenings can be one of the city's most useful layers.

planningPriorities.title: Do not overschedule heavy history blocks back to back
planningPriorities.description: The city works better when museum, monument, wall, and evening layers are balanced instead of stacked too tightly.

transportNote: Nanjing is easy to reach by high-speed rail from Shanghai and nearby east-China cities, but it usually rewards travelers who give the stop enough room for one evening and one full historical day.
heroImageAlt: Daytime view of the Qinhuai River in Nanjing with traditional riverside buildings and sightseeing boats under a bright blue sky.
